old_claim.note: Digital Signal Processing (DSP) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because the 1960 orbital optical-imaging anchor used television cameras, video recorders, ground readout, and film prints rather than digital signal processing as a prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: NOAA says TIROS-1 carried television cameras and video recorders; when data was read out, images were recorded on 35-mm film for prints.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The first scoped spaceborne optical instrument used analog television imaging and film workflows, not digital signal processing.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Digital processing is important for later remote sensing and astronomical analysis, but the source-backed 1960 orbital-imaging claim predates and does not require a DSP edge.

ontology_before: Space-Based Advanced Optics depended directly on Digital Signal Processing, pulling later digital analysis into the first spaceborne optical payload.
ontology_after: Spaceborne Optical Instruments is anchored to early spacecraft optical payloads; DSP remains available on downstream analysis-heavy nodes such as exoplanet detection.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: advanced_optics_space_based no longer directly depends on digital_signal_processing.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed to modern digital image reconstruction, interferometric processing, or exoplanet signal extraction.
A stronger source shows the 1960 anchor required onboard or ground digital signal processing.
A separate spaceborne_optical_data_processing node is added and should carry the DSP dependency.
short_reason: DSP is downstream analysis/scaling context, not a direct prerequisite for the 1960 TIROS optical payload.
